summary: Jeff Atwood surveys the history of educational programming games, from the c-jump board game to classic Apple II titles like Robot Odyssey and Rocky's Boots, through to the 2001 game Mindrover. He traces a lineage of games that taught programming concepts through puzzle-solving and robot programming, highlighting how these games made learning boolean logic and circuit design engaging. The post serves as both nostalgia trip and gentle criticism of Microsoft's then-new Coding4Fun initiative, pointing out that the tradition of making programming fun extends back decades before corporate efforts to brand it.
key_points: The Oregon Trail, while not a programming game, established the template for educational computer games in 1971
Robot Odyssey and Rocky's Boots taught real programming concepts (boolean logic, inputs/outputs, circuit design) through robot puzzle games
Warren Robinett, creator of Rocky's Boots, also created the Atari 2600 BASIC programming cartridge and the influential game Adventure
Mindrover (2001) carried forward the tradition of programming games into 3D graphics
Microsoft's Coding4Fun site was arriving 20 years late to a party that Apple II games had started
money_quotes: Although the prospect of a computer programming board game sounds positively stultifying, there is a rich history of computer games based on programming.

Robot Odyssey was about programming robots to solve puzzles. In order to do that, of course, you'd have to wire up the guts of the robot, and that meant learning about connecting inputs to outputs, and boolean logic.
It had one of the best tutorial modes in any game I've ever played, which was critical for a game this complex.
Microsoft may have just launched their Coding4Fun site a few months ago, but the real programming fun started 20 years earlier.
key_insight: Educational programming games that made learning genuinely fun existed decades before tech companies tried to brand 'coding as fun' as a novel concept.
